title: Wreck: three broken rules
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of fixing it. Bring the code back.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was. All three broken things are still there.
hint2.untouched: Look at the preview. None of the three rules works. One at a time.
why.not-closed: An HTML tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed: Check `<style>` and `</style>`. What's broken is the CSS, not the HTML — leave the other tags alone.
why.no-h1: There is no heading. You deleted the `<h1>` instead of fixing the rule for it.
hint2.no-h1: You're fixing styles. The HTML below is fine and stays untouched.
why.no-p: There is not a single paragraph left. You deleted them.
hint2.no-p: The two lines of text are fine. What's broken is above, between `<style>` and `</style>`.
why.no-background: The background isn't cream. The body rule is there, it looks fine — and it doesn't work.
hint2.no-background: Look at the end of the `background-color` line. Compare it with the end of the line below.
hint3.no-background: The semicolon ends a declaration. Without it the browser reads the next line as a continuation of the same value and throws both away. Then it seeks forward to the next semicolon or the closing brace and carries on from there.
why.no-font: The font isn't Georgia. This line is written correctly — it dies with the line above it.
hint2.no-font: This line isn't broken on its own. The one before it is.
hint3.no-font: The browser doesn't stop on an error. It quietly discards what it can't parse and moves on. That's how one missing semicolon kills two lines while you only see the result.
hint4.no-font: Fix the line above and this one fixes itself.
why.h1-not-blue: The heading isn't dark blue. The selector is right, the property is spelled correctly — and still nothing.
hint2.h1-not-blue: Read what comes after `color`. Is that a colour?
hint3.h1-not-blue: Every property accepts a certain kind of value. `color` wants a colour, `font-size` wants a size. Give a size to a colour and the browser throws the whole line away — silently, without a word.
hint4.h1-not-blue: Change the value to a colour. The heading should be `darkblue`.
why.p-not-brown: The paragraphs aren't brown. And the rule for `p` is written without a single mistake.
hint2.p-not-brown: If the rule is correct, the break is above it. Count the curly braces on the `h1` rule.
hint3.p-not-brown: An unclosed rule never ends. The next one lands INSIDE it and the browser reads it as "p inside h1". The rule is alive and running — and it matches nothing, because there is no paragraph inside a heading. That's why the preview stays quiet instead of complaining.
hint4.p-not-brown: Close the `h1` rule before the `p` rule starts.
why.lost-p: A paragraph is gone. You're fixing styles, not deleting text.
blocks.1.text: So far you've seen what a rule looks like. Now you'll see what a BROKEN rule looks like — and why that's the more useful skill. The owner ordered a design, someone wrote it in a hurry, and nothing shows up.
blocks.3.marks.0.label: selector
blocks.3.marks.1.label: property
blocks.3.marks.2.label: value
blocks.3.legend.0: The selector sits before the brace and has no colon after it. That is exactly how the browser tells it apart from a property.
blocks.3.legend.1: The semicolon closes the declaration. The last one in a block can do without it — until the day you add a line underneath.
blocks.4.text: Four parts: which elements, how far it reaches, what changes, and to what. Each of the four breaks in its own way, and not one of those breaks looks like a break.
blocks.6.text: Here's the important part of this lesson. The browser does NOT stop on a CSS error. No red, no message, no line number. It discards what it can't understand and carries on as if nothing happened.
blocks.7.items.0: Missing semicolon — you lose that line AND the next one.
blocks.7.items.1: Wrong kind of value — you lose just that line, quietly.
blocks.7.items.2: Missing curly brace — the next rule lands INSIDE the unclosed one and starts meaning something else.
blocks.7.items.3: Right kind of value, wrong number — you lose nothing. It gets applied exactly as written. That isn't an error to the browser, only to you.
blocks.8.text: The silence isn't malicious. It's deliberate: a page with one broken character should still render rather than go black. You're the one who pays for it, while you hunt.
blocks.10.text: There are three rules on the right and not one of them works. Three different broken things are keeping them dead — one each. Fix them. The background should be `cornsilk`, the font Georgia, the heading `darkblue`, the paragraphs `saddlebrown`.
blocks.13.items.0: A rule: selector, curly braces, inside them property, colon, value, semicolon.
blocks.13.items.1: Missing semicolon — two lines die, not one.
blocks.13.items.2: Missing closing brace — the next rule lands inside and turns into "this inside that".
blocks.13.items.3: Wrong kind of value — only that line dies. For all three the browser says nothing.
blocks.14.text: Hunt with your eyes while you still can. Later we'll show you the tool that points a finger at the line that was thrown away — lesson 55.
walkthrough.0.text: The semicolon after `cornsilk`. It isn't decoration — it's the end of the declaration. Without it, `background-color` and `font-family` are one single thing as far as the browser is concerned, and an invalid one.
walkthrough.1.text: `color: 20px` became `color: darkblue`. The property was spelled right, the braces were fine — the kind of value was wrong. A pixel isn't a colour, so the browser throws the whole line away.
walkthrough.2.text: The closing brace on `h1`. This is the only one of the three that isn't visible on the line where the problem shows. The `p` rule was flawless and dead anyway — it had landed inside the unclosed `h1` and meant "a paragraph inside a heading".
walkthrough.3.text: The order you fix them in doesn't matter — the three don't touch each other. The order you LOOK in does: braces first, then semicolons, then values. A brace breaks the most things at once. And before any of that — ask whether the rule is broken at all. A selector for a class that isn't on the page is perfectly valid CSS. It runs, and it matches nothing. A typo in a pseudo-class is not the same thing — that makes the selector invalid and throws away the whole block under it.
